summary: Stancil argues that data teams often overvalue objective truth-telling, when their most useful role in business may be to align people around a chosen strategy. Because execution and belief shape outcomes, selective, narrative-driven use of data can be more valuable than neutral analysis, even if it feels uncomfortable.
money_quotes: But, crass as it may sound, truth is just a means to an end—to make a company successful.
The question shouldn’t be if this is a useful role for a data team; the question should be, is it the most useful role for a data team?
The success of business decisions and strategies, by contrast, depend heavily on what happens after a decision is made.
In this light, it seems at least possible that the 76ers’ data team—and by extension, every data team—would be more valuable as periodic propagandists than they would be as truth seekers.
The point of the dashboard isn’t to make decisions. It’s to keep the mission at the front of everyone’s mind.
key_insight: In business, data’s highest value often lies not in objective truth-finding but in building conviction and alignment that make strategies succeed.
